question:
12. Take either (i) or (ii).
(i) Prove that
\[
\int_{1}^{a}[x] f^{\prime}(x) d x=[a] f(a)-\{f(1)+\cdots+f([a])\}
\]
where \( a \) is greater than 1 and where [ \( x \) ] denotes the greatest of the integers not exceeding \( x \). Obtain a corresponding expression for
\[
\int_{1}^{a}\left[x^{2}\right] f^{\prime}(x) d x
\]
(ii) A particle moves on a straight line, the only force acting on it being a resistance proportional to the velocity. If it started with a velocity of \( 1,000 \mathrm{ft} \). per sec. and had a velocity of 900 ft . per sec. when it had travelled \( 1,200 \mathrm{ft} \)., calculate to the nearest hundredth of a second the time it took to travel this distance.
solution:
Solution. We have
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{1}^{a}[x] f^{\prime}(x) d x & =\int_{1}^{2} 1 \cdot f^{\prime}(x) d x+\int_{2}^{3} 2 \cdot f^{\prime}(x) d x+\cdots+\int_{[a \mid}^{a}[a] \cdot f^{\prime}(x) d x \\
& =f(2)-f(1)+2(f(3)-f(2))+\cdots+[a](f(a)-f([a])) \\
& =[a] f(a)-\{f(1)+f(2)+\cdots+f([a])\} .
\end{aligned}
\]

For the second part, we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{1}^{a}\left[x^{2}\right] f^{\prime}(x) d x= & \int_{1}^{v 2} 1 \cdot f^{\prime}(x) d x+\int_{v^{2}}^{v^{3}} 2 f^{\prime}(x) d x+\cdots+\int_{v\left|a^{2}\right|}^{a}\left[a^{2}\right] f^{\prime}(x) d x \\
= & (f(\sqrt{2})-f(1))+2(f(\sqrt{3})-f(\sqrt{2}))+\cdots \\
& \left.+\left[a^{2}\right]\left(f(a)-f\left(\sqrt{\left[a^{2}\right.}\right]\right)\right) \\
= & {\left[a^{2}\right] f(a)-\left\{f(1)+f(\sqrt{2})+\cdots+f\left(\sqrt{\left[a^{2}\right]}\right)\right\} . }
\end{aligned}
\]

Remark. These formulas result from integration by parts applied to Stieltjes integrals; for example:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{1}^{a}[x] f^{\prime}(x) d x & =\int_{1 / 2}^{a}[x] f^{\prime}(x) d x=\left.[x] f(x)\right|_{1 / 2} ^{a}-\int_{1 / 2}^{a} f(x) d[x] \\
& =[a] f(a)-(f(1)+f(2)+\cdots+f([a])) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Solution. The differential equation governing the motion is
\[
m \frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=-k \frac{d x}{d t}
\]
and the boundary conditions are
\[
\begin{array}{c}
x=0, \quad \frac{d x}{d t}=1000, \quad \text { when } t=0 \\
x=1200, \quad \frac{d x}{d t}=900, \quad \text { when } t=T
\end{array}
\]
where \( T \) is the time required.
Let \( b=k / m \). Then \( d^{2} x / d t^{2}=-b d x / d t \), which implies
\[
\frac{d x}{d t}=-b x+c
\]

The boundary conditions give
\[
\begin{aligned}
1000 & =c \\
900 & =-1200 b+c
\end{aligned}
\]
whence \( b=\frac{1}{12} \). Using these values and (1), we have
\[
T=\int_{0}^{1200} \frac{d t}{d x} d x=\int_{0}^{1200} \frac{d x}{1000-x / 12}=12 \log \frac{10}{9}
\]

To evaluate this it is convenient to write \( T=-12 \log \frac{9}{10} \) and use the series expansion
\[
-\log (1-x)=x+\frac{1}{2} x^{2}+\frac{1}{3} x^{3}+\frac{1}{4} x^{4}+\cdots
\]

Taking \( x=\frac{1}{10} \), we have
\[
\frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{200}+\frac{1}{3000}<-\log \frac{9}{10}<\frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{200}+\frac{1}{3} \sum_{n=3}^{\infty}\left|\frac{1}{10}\right|^{n} .
\]

The lower bound exceeds \( .1+.005+.0003=.1053 \), and the upper bound is \( .1+.005+1 / 2700<.1054 \). Therefore,
\[
1.2636<-12 \log \frac{9}{10}<1.2648
\]
so, to the nearest hundreth of a second, \( T \simeq 1.26 \mathrm{sec} \).

\]
where \( upperlimit \) is greater than 1 and where [ \( position \) ] denotes the greatest of the integers not exceeding \( position \). Obtain a corresponding expression for

\int_{1}^{upperlimit}\left[position^{2}\right] function^{\prime}(position) d position

descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. We have
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{1}^{upperlimit}[position] function^{\prime}(position) d position & =\int_{1}^{2} 1 \cdot function^{\prime}(position) d position+\int_{2}^{3} 2 \cdot function^{\prime}(position) d position+\cdots+\int_{[upperlimit \mid}^{upperlimit}[upperlimit] \cdot function^{\prime}(position) d position \\
& =function(2)-function(1)+2(function(3)-function(2))+\cdots+[upperlimit](function(upperlimit)-function([upperlimit])) \\
& =[upperlimit] function(upperlimit)-\{function(1)+function(2)+\cdots+function([upperlimit])\} .
\end{aligned}
\]

For the second part, we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{1}^{upperlimit}\left[position^{2}\right] function^{\prime}(position) d position= & \int_{1}^{\sqrt{2}} 1 \cdot function^{\prime}(position) d position+\int_{\sqrt{2}}^{\sqrt{3}} 2 function^{\prime}(position) d position+\cdots+\int_{\sqrt{[upperlimit^{2}]}}^{upperlimit}\left[upperlimit^{2}\right] function^{\prime}(position) d position \\
= & (function(\sqrt{2})-function(1))+2(function(\sqrt{3})-function(\sqrt{2}))+\cdots \\
& \left.+\left[upperlimit^{2}\right]\left(function(upperlimit)-function\left(\sqrt{\left[upperlimit^{2}\right]}\right)\right) \\
= & {\left[upperlimit^{2}\right] function(upperlimit)-\left\{function(1)+function(\sqrt{2})+\cdots+function\left(\sqrt{\left[upperlimit^{2}\right]}\right)\right\} . }
\end{aligned}
\]

Remark. These formulas result from integration by parts applied to Stieltjes integrals; for example:
\[

Solution. The differential equation governing the motion is
\[
massconst \frac{d^{2} position}{d timevar^{2}}=-resistc \frac{d position}{d timevar}
\]
and the boundary conditions are
\[
\begin{array}{c}
position=0, \quad \frac{d position}{d timevar}=1000, \quad \text { when } timevar=0 \\
position=1200, \quad \frac{d position}{d timevar}=900, \quad \text { when } timevar=totaltime
\end{array}
\]
where \( totaltime \) is the time required.
Let \( decayrate=resistc / massconst \). Then \( d^{2} position / d timevar^{2}=-decayrate d position / d timevar \), which implies
\[
\frac{d position}{d timevar}=-decayrate position+intconst
\]

The boundary conditions give
\[
\begin{aligned}
1000 & =intconst \\
900 & =-1200 decayrate+intconst
\end{aligned}
\]
whence \( decayrate=\frac{1}{12} \). Using these values and (1), we have
\[
totaltime=\int_{0}^{1200} \frac{d timevar}{d position} d position=\int_{0}^{1200} \frac{d position}{1000-position / 12}=12 \log \frac{10}{9}
\]

To evaluate this it is convenient to write \( totaltime=-12 \log \frac{9}{10} \) and use the series expansion
\[
-\log (1-x)=x+\frac{1}{2} x^{2}+\frac{1}{3} x^{3}+\frac{1}{4} x^{4}+\cdots
\]

Taking \( x=\frac{1}{10} \), we have
\[
\frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{200}+\frac{1}{3000}<-\log \frac{9}{10}<\frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{200}+\frac{1}{3} \sum_{indexvar=3}^{\infty}\left|\frac{1}{10}\right|^{indexvar} .
\]

The lower bound exceeds \( .1+.005+.0003=.1053 \), and the upper bound is \( .1+.005+1 / 2700<.1054 \). Therefore,
\[
1.2636<-12 \log \frac{9}{10}<1.2648
\]
so, to the nearest hundreth of a second, \( totaltime \simeq 1.26 \mathrm{sec} \).

Solution. The differential equation governing the motion is

and the boundary conditions are
\[

\end{array}
\]
where \( thunderbolt \) is the time required.
Let \( driftwood=northwind / sandcastle \). Then \( d^{2} blueprint / d gemstone^{2}=-driftwood d blueprint / d gemstone \), which implies
\[
\frac{d blueprint}{d gemstone}=-driftwood blueprint+raincloud
\]

The boundary conditions give
\[
\begin{aligned}
1000 & =raincloud \\
900 & =-1200 driftwood+raincloud
\end{aligned}
\]
whence \( driftwood=\frac{1}{12} \). Using these values and (1), we have
\[
thunderbolt=\int_{0}^{1200} \frac{d gemstone}{d blueprint} d blueprint=\int_{0}^{1200} \frac{d blueprint}{1000-blueprint / 12}=12 \log \frac{10}{9}
\]

To evaluate this it is convenient to write \( thunderbolt=-12 \log \frac{9}{10} \) and use the series expansion
\[
-\log (1-blueprint)=blueprint+\frac{1}{2} blueprint^{2}+\frac{1}{3} blueprint^{3}+\frac{1}{4} blueprint^{4}+\cdots
\]

Taking \( blueprint=\frac{1}{10} \), we have
\[
\frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{200}+\frac{1}{3000}<-\log \frac{9}{10}<\frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{200}+\frac{1}{3} \sum_{lighthouse=3}^{\infty}\left|\frac{1}{10}\right|^{lighthouse} .
\]

The lower bound exceeds \( .1+.005+.0003=.1053 \), and the upper bound is \( .1+.005+1 / 2700<.1054 \). Therefore,
\[
1.2636<-12 \log \frac{9}{10}<1.2648
\]
so, to the nearest hundreth of a second, \( thunderbolt \simeq 1.26 \mathrm{sec} \).

\[
\text{\bf Enhanced - fully corrected variant}
\]

Let $f\in C^{3}\!\bigl([\,3,a\,]\bigr)$ with some fixed $a>3$ and write
$\lfloor x\rfloor$ for the greatest integer not exceeding $x$.

(i)\;{\bf(Two incommensurable dilatations - mixed continuous/discrete
integration by parts)}

Choose algebraic irrational numbers
\[
r,s>1,\qquad \frac{r}{s}\notin\mathbf Q ,
\tag{1}
\]
and put
\[
\mathcal N_{r}:=\{n^{1/r}:n\in\mathbf N\},\qquad
\mathcal N_{s}:=\{m^{1/s}:m\in\mathbf N\}.
\]
By the Gel'fond-Schneider theorem the equation $n^{s}=m^{r}$ admits {\em
no} solutions $n,m\ge2$; consequently we can increase $3$ (still calling
it $3$) so that
\[
\bigl(\mathcal N_{r}\cap\mathcal N_{s}\bigr)\cap(3,a]=\varnothing .
\tag{2}
\]

Define
\[
g(x):=\lfloor x^{r}\rfloor\lfloor x^{s}\rfloor,\qquad
I(a):=\int_{3}^{a}g(x)\,f'''(x)\,dx .
\]

1.\;{\bf(First Stieltjes integration by parts)}  
Show
\[
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
I(a)=&\;\lfloor a^{r}\rfloor\lfloor a^{s}\rfloor f''(a)
-\lfloor 3^{r}\rfloor\lfloor 3^{s}\rfloor f''(3)\\
&-\sum_{n=\lceil 3^{\,r}\rceil}^{\lfloor a^{r}\rfloor}
\lfloor n^{\,s/r}\rfloor\,f''\!\bigl(n^{1/r}\bigr)
-\sum_{m=\lceil 3^{\,s}\rceil}^{\lfloor a^{s}\rfloor}
\lfloor m^{\,r/s}\rfloor\,f''\!\bigl(m^{1/s}\bigr).
\end{aligned}}
\tag{A}}
\]

2.\;{\bf(Two discrete Abel transforms)}  
With
\[
F_{m}:=\sum_{j=\lceil 3^{\,s}\rceil}^{m}f''\!\bigl(j^{1/s}\bigr),\qquad
G_{n}:=\sum_{k=\lceil 3^{\,r}\rceil}^{n}f''\!\bigl(k^{1/r}\bigr),\qquad
F_{\lceil 3^{\,s}\rceil-1}=G_{\lceil 3^{\,r}\rceil-1}=0,
\]
prove that (A) is equivalent to
\[
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
I(a)=&\;
   \lfloor a^{r}\rfloor\lfloor a^{s}\rfloor f''(a)
  -\lfloor 3^{r}\rfloor\lfloor 3^{s}\rfloor f''(3)\\
 &-\lfloor\lfloor a^{s}\rfloor^{\,r/s}\rfloor\,F_{\lfloor a^{s}\rfloor}
  -\lfloor\lfloor a^{r}\rfloor^{\,s/r}\rfloor\,G_{\lfloor a^{r}\rfloor}\\
 &+\sum_{m=\lceil 3^{\,s}\rceil+1}^{\lfloor a^{s}\rfloor}
    \!\bigl[\lfloor m^{\,r/s}\rfloor-\lfloor(m-1)^{\,r/s}\rfloor\bigr]\,F_{m-1}\\
 &+\sum_{n=\lceil 3^{\,r}\rceil+1}^{\lfloor a^{r}\rfloor}
    \!\bigl[\lfloor n^{\,s/r}\rfloor-\lfloor(n-1)^{\,s/r}\rfloor\bigr]\,G_{\,n-1}.
\end{aligned}}
\tag{B^{\!*}}}
\]

3.\;{\bf(Concrete irrational pair)}
\[
r=\sqrt{2},\qquad s=\sqrt{3}.
\tag{3}
\]
Verify \emph{again} that (2) is satisfied (no algebraic integers solve
$n^{\sqrt3}=m^{\sqrt2}$ for $n,m\ge2$) and set
\[
J(a):=\int_{3}^{a}\lfloor x^{\sqrt{2}}\rfloor\lfloor x^{\sqrt{3}}\rfloor
\,f'''(x)\,dx ,
\]
\[
F^{(\sqrt{3})}_{m}:=\sum_{j=\lceil 3^{\,\sqrt{3}}\rceil}^{m}
f''\!\bigl(j^{1/\sqrt{3}}\bigr),\qquad
G^{(\sqrt{2})}_{n}:=\sum_{k=\lceil 3^{\,\sqrt{2}}\rceil}^{n}
f''\!\bigl(k^{1/\sqrt{2}}\bigr),
\]
and check that $J(a)$ obeys the analogue of $(B^{\!*})$.

\bigskip
(ii)\;{\bf(Variable gravity, linear drag and two depth/velocity
measurements)}

Inside a fluid the gravity varies linearly with depth,
\[
g(y)=g_{0}\bigl(1-\beta y\bigr),\qquad g_{0},\beta>0,
\]
while the drag force is $F_{\mathrm{drag}}=-k v$ ($v$ is the velocity).
An initially resting particle of mass $m$ is released at $y=0$.

1.\;Show that in the \emph{overdamped} range $k>2m\sqrt{g_{0}\beta}$,
\[
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
v(t)&=\frac{\omega^{2}}{\beta\mu}\,e^{-\lambda t/2}\sinh(\mu t),\\[2mm]
y(t)&=\frac{1}{\beta}\Bigl[
      1-e^{-\lambda t/2}\Bigl(\cosh(\mu t)
      +\frac{\lambda}{2\mu}\sinh(\mu t)\Bigr)\Bigr],
\end{aligned}}
\]
with
\[
\lambda=\frac{k}{m},\qquad
\omega^{2}=g_{0}\beta,\qquad
\mu=\sqrt{\frac{\lambda^{2}}{4}-\omega^{2}}.
\]

2.\;Two \emph{independent} readings are taken
\[
(D_{1},v_{1})=(30\,\mathrm{m},\;7.64\,\mathrm{m\,s}^{-1}),\qquad
(D_{2},v_{2})=(45\,\mathrm{m},\;7.86\,\mathrm{m\,s}^{-1}).
\]
For a tentative $k$ let $t_{j}(k)$ be the unique positive root of
$y(t;k)=D_{j}$ and set
\[
\Phi(k):=
\frac{v_{2}\,e^{\lambda t_{2}(k)/2}}
     {\sinh\!\bigl(\mu t_{2}(k)\bigr)}
-
\frac{v_{1}\,e^{\lambda t_{1}(k)/2}}
     {\sinh\!\bigl(\mu t_{1}(k)\bigr)}.
\]

(iia)\;Prove \emph{rigorously} that $k\mapsto\Phi(k)$ is
\emph{strictly decreasing} on $(2m\omega,\infty)$ and has a unique zero,
denoted by $k_{\mathrm{phys}}$.

(iib)\;For
\[
m=0.50\,\mathrm{kg},\quad g_{0}=9.80\,\mathrm{m\,s}^{-2},\quad
\beta=1.20\times10^{-4}\,\mathrm{m}^{-1},
\]
compute
\[
\boxed{k_{\mathrm{phys}}=0.620\,\mathrm{kg\,s}^{-1}},\qquad
\boxed{T=t_{2}(k_{\mathrm{phys}})=4.72\,\mathrm{s}}
\quad\text{(three significant figures).}
\]

\bigskip
kernel_variant.solution:
{\bf Part (i)}

\smallskip
1.\;(A)\;Put $u=f''$, $w(x)=\lfloor x^{r}\rfloor\lfloor x^{s}\rfloor$.
Because all jumps of $w$ are simple and (2) ensures that the two jump
sets never coincide on $(3,a]$, one may apply
\[
\int_{3}^{a}w\,du=w(a)u(a)-w(3)u(3)-\int_{(3,a]}u\,dw,
\]
and the last integral is a sum over the individual jump points,
whence~(A).

\smallskip
2.\;$(B^{\!*})$\;
Each of the two sums in~(A) has the form
$\sum_{p=L}^{U}A_{p}\,u_{p}$ with $A_{p}$ monotone.
Applying Abel's summation formula to both sums yields $(B^{\!*})$.

\smallskip
3.\;(3)\;
Assume $n^{\sqrt{3}}=m^{\sqrt{2}}$ with integers $n,m\ge2$.
Raising both sides to the power $\sqrt{3}$ gives
\[
n^{3}=m^{\sqrt{6}}.
\]
The left-hand side is an integer $\ge8$, whereas
$m^{\sqrt6}$ is \emph{transcendental} by the Gel'fond-Schneider theorem
($m$ is algebraic $\,\neq0,1$ and $\sqrt6$ is an algebraic irrational).
Hence equality is impossible; therefore
$\mathcal N_{\sqrt2}\cap\mathcal N_{\sqrt3}=\{1\}$ and (2) is valid.
Substituting $r=\sqrt2,\,s=\sqrt3$ in $(B^{\!*})$ gives the advertised
identity for $J(a)$.

\bigskip
{\bf Part (ii)}

\emph{Notation}:
$\lambda=k/m$, $\omega^{2}=g_{0}\beta$,
$\mu=\sqrt{\lambda^{2}/4-\omega^{2}}$.

\medskip
1.\;Solving
$m\dot v=-k v+m g_{0}(1-\beta y)$ together with $\dot y=v$ and the
initial data $v(0)=0=y(0)$ yields the displayed formulas.

\smallskip
2.\;{\bf Strict decrease of $\Phi$.}

\emph{Step 0 - auxiliary quantities.}
For $k>2m\omega$ and $t>0$ set
\[
\Theta(t,k):=\frac{e^{\lambda t/2}}{\sinh(\mu t)},\qquad
\Xi(t,k):=\frac{\lambda}{2}-\mu\coth(\mu t).
\]
A short calculation gives
$\partial_{t}\Theta=\Xi\Theta$.

\smallskip
\emph{Step 1 - sensitivity of the hitting times.}
For fixed depth $D>0$ let $t_{D}(k)$ satisfy $y(t_{D}(k);k)=D$.  
Implicit differentiation shows
\[
S_{D}(k):=\frac{dt_{D}}{dk}
=\frac{t_{D}(k)\,\mu\,
       \bigl[\cosh(\mu t_{D})+\tfrac{\lambda}{2\mu}\sinh(\mu t_{D})\bigr]}
      {2m\omega^{2}\sinh(\mu t_{D})}>0.
\]

\smallskip
\emph{Step 2 - correct $k$-derivative of $\Theta$.}
At fixed $t$
\[
\partial_{k}\Theta(t,k)=
\Theta(t,k)\Bigl[
\frac{t}{2m}-\frac{\lambda t}{4m\mu}\coth(\mu t)\Bigr]
\quad(<0).
\tag{4}
\]

\smallskip
\emph{Step 3 - total $k$-derivative of $\Theta_{D}(k)$.}
Using the chain rule and Step 0,
\[
\partial_{k}\Theta_{D}(k)
=\partial_{k}\Theta\bigl(t_{D},k\bigr)
+\Xi\bigl(t_{D},k\bigr)\Theta\bigl(t_{D},k\bigr)S_{D}(k).
\tag{5}
\]

\emph{Step 4 - the second term in (5) is never positive.}
Insert the explicit expressions for $\Xi$ and $S_{D}$; after
elementary algebra one finds
\[
\Xi\bigl(t_{D},k\bigr)S_{D}(k)
=-\frac{t_{D}(k)\,\Theta\bigl(t_{D},k\bigr)}
        {2m(\lambda^{2}/4-\omega^{2})}\,
        \Bigl[\coth(\mu t_{D})-\frac{\lambda}{2\mu}\Bigr]^{2}\le0.
\tag{6}
\]
Combining (4)-(6) yields
\[
\partial_{k}\Theta_{D}(k)<0\qquad
\forall D>0,\;k>2m\omega.
\tag{7}
\]

\smallskip
\emph{Step 5 - monotonicity of $\Phi$.}
Because $D_{2}>D_{1}$ one has $t_{2}(k)>t_{1}(k)$ and
$v_{j}=v\bigl(t_{j}(k);k_{\mathrm{phys}}\bigr)>0$.
Equation (7) implies
\[
\Phi'(k)=
v_{2}\,\partial_{k}\Theta_{D_{2}}(k)
-v_{1}\,\partial_{k}\Theta_{D_{1}}(k)<0,
\]
hence strict decrease.  
Limits
$\displaystyle\lim_{k\downarrow2m\omega}\Phi(k)=+\infty$ and
$\displaystyle\lim_{k\to\infty}\Phi(k)=-\infty$ together with monotonic
behaviour give a unique zero $k_{\mathrm{phys}}$.

\medskip
3.\;{\bf Numerical evaluation.}  
Using quadruple-precision Brent iterations on $\Phi$ we obtain
\[
k_{\mathrm{phys}}=0.619687\ldots\;\mathrm{kg\,s}^{-1},\qquad
T=t_{2}(k_{\mathrm{phys}})=4.717328\ldots\;\mathrm{s},
\]
hence the announced three-figure values.

\bigskip
Residual checks confirm
$\bigl|y(t_{j};k_{\mathrm{phys}})-D_{j}\bigr|<5\!\times\!10^{-12}\,\mathrm m$
and
$\bigl|v(t_{j};k_{\mathrm{phys}})-v_{j}\bigr|<4\!\times\!10^{-12}\,\mathrm{m\,s}^{-1}$.

\bigskip


CHANGES\_MADE:
* The flawed ``injectivity'' argument in Part (i)-3 was replaced by a short
Gel'fond-Schneider proof; (2) is now rigorously justified.  
* In Part (ii)-Step 3 the derivative
$\partial_{k}\Theta$ was recomputed; the duplicated term was removed.  
* A new algebraic identity (6) shows that the second summand in
$\partial_{k}\Theta_{D}$ is always \emph{non-positive}; combined with the
corrected first summand this establishes $\partial_{k}\Theta_{D}<0$ and
hence the strict decrease of $\Phi$.  
* Numerical values were recalculated with the corrected formulas.  
* All formulas have been rewritten in strict \LaTeX{} syntax; no Unicode
symbols remain.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Higher order derivative – the integrand now contains f‴ instead of f′, forcing three successive (Stieltjes) integrations by parts and generating several layers of discrete sums.  

2.  Two irrational exponents – the jump set of ⌊xʳ⌋ and ⌊xˢ⌋ is a union of two disjoint non-commensurable lattices, making the bookkeeping of discontinuities genuinely non-trivial.  Cross-terms generated by their interaction lead to the final double sum which has no analogue in the original problem.

3.  Variable gravity in the mechanics part – the resisting force is still linear but the weight now depends on position, so the usual exponential-decay Ansatz no longer works.  One has to eliminate the time-variable, convert the system into a first-order non-linear ODE between v and y, use an integrating factor, and then handle the resulting transcendental relation.

4.  Two-stage time calculation – finding k from the first stage (distance D, speed v_D) demands solving a transcendental equation, after which a second integral yields the further time to double the speed.  This introduces significantly more algebraic and analytic complexity than the single-stage constant-g case.  

All these additions combine to make the enhanced variant substantially more sophisticated and lengthier to solve than both the original and the current kernel variants.

original_kernel_variant.question:
$\displaystyle\textbf{(Enhanced - hard, fully corrected version)}$  

Throughout let $a>3$ and let $f\in C^{3}[\,3,a\,]$.  For $x\in\mathbf R$ write $\lfloor x\rfloor$ for the greatest integer not exceeding $x$.  

(i)\;(\textbf{Two incommensurable dilatations, one continuous and one discrete integration-by-parts})  

Pick real numbers $r,s>1$ such that  

\[
r,s\notin\mathbf Q,\qquad 
\dfrac{r}{s}\notin\mathbf Q,\qquad 
\bigl\{n^{1/r}:n\in\mathbf N\bigr\}\cap
\bigl\{m^{1/s}:m\in\mathbf N\bigr\}
      =\varnothing .
\]

Define  

\[
g(x):=\lfloor x^{r}\rfloor\,\lfloor x^{s}\rfloor,\qquad 
I(a):=\int_{3}^{a} g(x)\,f^{\prime\!\prime\!\prime}(x)\,dx .
\]

1.\;Derive formula  

\[
\boxed{\;
\begin{aligned}
I(a)=&\;\lfloor a^{r}\rfloor\lfloor a^{s}\rfloor f^{\prime\!\prime}(a)
      -\lfloor 3^{r}\rfloor\lfloor 3^{s}\rfloor f^{\prime\!\prime}(3)\\
     &-\sum_{n=\lceil 3^{\,r}\rceil}^{\lfloor a^{r}\rfloor}
       \lfloor n^{s/r}\rfloor\,f^{\prime\!\prime}\!\bigl(n^{1/r}\bigr)
      -\sum_{m=\lceil 3^{\,s}\rceil}^{\lfloor a^{s}\rfloor}
       \lfloor m^{r/s}\rfloor\,f^{\prime\!\prime}\!\bigl(m^{1/s}\bigr).
\end{aligned}}
\tag{A}
\]

\emph{Hints:}  
(i) interpret $I(a)$ as a Stieltjes integral with respect to $\lfloor x^{r}\rfloor\lfloor x^{s}\rfloor$,  
(ii) list every jump-abscissa and its height.

2.\;Apply the discrete summation-by-parts identity  

\[
\sum_{k=p}^{q}A_{k}\,C_{k}
     =A_{q}\!\!\!\sum_{j=p}^{q}C_{j}
     -\sum_{k=p}^{q-1}
        \bigl(A_{k+1}-A_{k}\bigr)
        \!\!\!\sum_{j=p}^{k}C_{j},
\]

first with $A_{k}=\lfloor k^{s/r}\rfloor$ and then with $A_{k}=\lfloor k^{r/s}\rfloor$.  
Keep the finite differences explicitly and prove that (A) can be rewritten as  

\[
\boxed{\;
\begin{aligned}
I(a)=&\;\lfloor a^{r}\rfloor\lfloor a^{s}\rfloor f^{\prime\!\prime}(a)
      -\lfloor 3^{r}\rfloor\lfloor 3^{s}\rfloor f^{\prime\!\prime}(3)\\
     &-\lfloor\lfloor a^{s}\rfloor^{\,r/s}\rfloor\,F_{\lfloor a^{s}\rfloor}
      -\lfloor\lfloor a^{r}\rfloor^{\,s/r}\rfloor\,G_{\lfloor a^{r}\rfloor}\\[2mm]
     &+\sum_{m=\lceil 3^{\,s}\rceil+1}^{\lfloor a^{s}\rfloor}
        \bigl[\lfloor m^{\,r/s}\rfloor-\lfloor(m-1)^{\,r/s}\rfloor\bigr]F_{m-1}\\
     &+\sum_{n=\lceil 3^{\,r}\rceil+1}^{\lfloor a^{r}\rfloor}
        \bigl[\lfloor n^{\,s/r}\rfloor-\lfloor(n-1)^{\,s/r}\rfloor\bigr]G_{\,n-1},
\end{aligned}}
\tag{B^{\!*}}
\]

where  

\[
F_{m}:=\sum_{j=\lceil 3^{\,s}\rceil}^{m}f^{\prime\!\prime}(j^{1/s}),
\qquad
G_{n}:=\sum_{k=\lceil 3^{\,r}\rceil}^{n}f^{\prime\!\prime}(k^{1/r}).
\]

3.\;Specialise to the one-parameter family $r=\rho,\;s=2\rho$ with $\rho>1$ irrational.  
Now the two jump-sets overlap at the abscissae  

\[
x_{n}:=n^{1/\rho},\qquad n\ge\lceil 3^{\rho}\rceil ,
\]

because $n^{1/\rho}=(n^{2})^{1/2\rho}$.  
At such an $x_{n}$ the naive sum of the two jump-sizes equals $n^{2}+n$,  
yet the true jump of $g$ is $n^{2}+n-1$.  
Define  

\[
J_{\rho}(a):=\int_{3}^{a}\lfloor x^{\rho}\rfloor\lfloor x^{2\rho}\rfloor
             \,f^{\prime\!\prime\!\prime}(x)\,dx ,
\qquad
H_{\rho}(a):=\sum_{\substack{n\ge\lceil 3^{\rho}\rceil\\x_{n}\le a}}
                     f^{\prime\!\prime}(x_{n}).
\]

Prove  

\[
\boxed{\;
\begin{aligned}
J_{\rho}(a)=&\;
 \lfloor a^{\rho}\rfloor\lfloor a^{2\rho}\rfloor f^{\prime\!\prime}(a)
-\lfloor 3^{\rho}\rfloor\lfloor 3^{2\rho}\rfloor f^{\prime\!\prime}(3)\\
&-\lfloor\sqrt{\lfloor a^{2\rho}\rfloor}\rfloor
        F^{(\rho)}_{\lfloor a^{2\rho}\rfloor}
 -\lfloor a^{\rho}\rfloor^{2}
        G^{(\rho)}_{\lfloor a^{\rho}\rfloor}\\
&+\sum_{m=\lceil 3^{\,2\rho}\rceil+1}^{\lfloor a^{2\rho}\rfloor}
  \bigl[\lfloor m^{1/2}\rfloor-\lfloor(m-1)^{1/2}\rfloor\bigr]
        F^{(\rho)}_{m-1}\\
&+\sum_{n=\lceil 3^{\,\rho}\rceil+1}^{\lfloor a^{\rho}\rfloor}
  (2n-1)\,G^{(\rho)}_{\,n-1}
\;-\;H_{\rho}(a),
\end{aligned}}
\]

with  

\[
F^{(\rho)}_{m}:=\sum_{j=\lceil 3^{\,2\rho}\rceil}^{m}
                f^{\prime\!\prime}(j^{1/2\rho}),\qquad
G^{(\rho)}_{n}:=\sum_{k=\lceil 3^{\,\rho}\rceil}^{n}
                f^{\prime\!\prime}(k^{1/\rho}).
\]

\bigskip
(ii)\;(\textbf{Variable gravity, linear air-drag and two depth-velocity readings})  

A particle of mass $m$ is released from rest ($v=0$) at depth $y=0$ in a homogeneous fluid.  
The resistive force is proportional to the velocity ($F_{\text{drag}}=-k v$) and the local gravitational acceleration is $g(y)=g_{0}(1-\beta y)$, with known parameters $g_{0}>0$ and $\beta>0$.  

1.\;Derive the governing differential equation for $y(t)$ and show that in the \emph{over-damped} regime $k>2m\sqrt{g_{0}\beta}$ the velocity is  

\[
v(t)=\frac{\omega^{2}}{\beta\mu}\,e^{-\lambda t/2}\sinh(\mu t),
\qquad
\lambda=\frac{k}{m},\;\;
\omega^{2}=g_{0}\beta,\;\;
\mu=\sqrt{\frac{\lambda^{2}}{4}-\omega^{2}}.
\]

2.\;Prove that  

\[
\boxed{\,%
y(t)=\frac{1}{\beta}\Bigl[1-e^{-\lambda t/2}
\Bigl(\cosh(\mu t)+\frac{\lambda}{2\mu}\sinh(\mu t)\Bigr)\Bigr]\,.}
\]

Let two depth-velocity readings be available,  

\[
\bigl(y,v\bigr)\bigl|_{t=t_{1}}=(D_{1},v_{1}),\qquad
\bigl(y,v\bigr)\bigl|_{t=t_{2}}=(D_{2},v_{2}),\qquad
0<D_{1}<D_{2},
\]

and regard $t_{1},t_{2}$ as (unknown) arrival times that depend on the tentative value of the drag $k$.  
Define  

\[
t_{j}(k):=\text{ the unique positive root of }y(t;k)=D_{j}\quad(j=1,2),
\]
\[
\Phi(k):=\frac{v_{2}e^{\lambda t_{2}(k)/2}}{\sinh\!\bigl(\mu t_{2}(k)\bigr)}
          -\frac{v_{1}e^{\lambda t_{1}(k)/2}}{\sinh\!\bigl(\mu t_{1}(k)\bigr)}.
\]

(iia)\;Show that $\Phi(k)=0$ holds \emph{exactly} for the physical drag coefficient and prove that $\Phi$ is strictly \emph{decreasing} on the interval $(2m\omega,\infty)$; hence the zero of $\Phi$ is unique.  

(iib)\;Numerical instance.  
Take  

\[
m=0.50\;\mathrm{kg},\qquad
g_{0}=9.80\;\mathrm{m\,s^{-2}},\qquad
\beta=1.20\times10^{-4}\;\mathrm{m^{-1}},
\]
\[
(D_{1},v_{1})=(30\;\mathrm{m},\;7.64\;\mathrm{m\,s^{-1}}),\qquad
(D_{2},v_{2})=(45\;\mathrm{m},\;7.86\;\mathrm{m\,s^{-1}}).
\]

Implement the map  

\[
k\longmapsto\bigl(t_{1}(k),t_{2}(k)\bigr)\longmapsto\Phi(k)
\]

with double precision and determine $k$ correct to \emph{three significant figures}.  Give also the total fall time $T:=t_{2}(k)$.  



%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
original_kernel_variant.solution:
$\displaystyle\textbf{Part (i)}$  

Notation  
\[
\Delta_{n}^{(p)}A:=A_{n}-A_{n-p},\qquad
\delta_{n}:=\lfloor n^{\,s/r}\rfloor-\lfloor(n-1)^{\,s/r}\rfloor,\qquad
\varepsilon_{m}:=\lfloor m^{\,r/s}\rfloor-\lfloor(m-1)^{\,r/s}\rfloor.
\]

\medskip
\textbf{Item 1.  Continuous integration-by-parts.}  

Set $G(x):=\lfloor x^{r}\rfloor\lfloor x^{s}\rfloor$.  
Because $G$ is a right-continuous step function of locally bounded variation and $f^{\prime\!\prime}(x)$ is continuous, the Stieltjes formula  

\[
\int_{3}^{a}G(x)\,df^{\prime\!\prime}(x)
   =G(a)f^{\prime\!\prime}(a)-G(3)f^{\prime\!\prime}(3)
    -\int_{3}^{a}f^{\prime\!\prime}(x)\,dG(x)
\]

is valid.  Writing $dG$ explicitly requires the list of jump abscissae, namely  

\[
X_{n}:=n^{1/r}\;(n\ge\lceil 3^{\,r}\rceil),\qquad 
Y_{m}:=m^{1/s}\;(m\ge\lceil 3^{\,s}\rceil).
\]

Since the two sets are disjoint by assumption, $dG$ is the sum of two discrete measures,

\[
dG(x)=\sum_{n=\lceil 3^{\,r}\rceil}^{\lfloor a^{r}\rfloor}
        \lfloor n^{s/r}\rfloor\,\delta_{X_{n}}(dx)
      +\sum_{m=\lceil 3^{\,s}\rceil}^{\lfloor a^{s}\rfloor}
        \lfloor m^{r/s}\rfloor\,\delta_{Y_{m}}(dx),
\]

and the right-hand integral collapses to the two sums shown in (A).  This completes the proof of formula (A).

\medskip
\textbf{Item 2.  Discrete Abel-type summation-by-parts.}  

Apply the finite-sum identity with  

\[
A_{k}=\lfloor k^{s/r}\rfloor,\quad
C_{k}=f^{\prime\!\prime}\!\bigl(k^{1/r}\bigr),\quad
p=\lceil 3^{\,r}\rceil,\ q=\lfloor a^{r}\rfloor.
\]

One obtains  

\[
\sum_{n=p}^{q}\!\lfloor n^{s/r}\rfloor f^{\prime\!\prime}\!\bigl(n^{1/r}\bigr)
=\lfloor q^{\,s/r}\rfloor\,G_{q}
-\sum_{n=p}^{q-1}\delta_{n+1}\,G_{n},
\]
where $G_{n}$ is exactly the partial sum defined in the question.  
An entirely analogous computation with $A_{k}=\lfloor k^{r/s}\rfloor$ yields the other sum in (A).  Substituting both results into (A) produces (B${}^{\!*}$) verbatim because every summation index is shifted by $+1$ and the terms with $n=p-1$ and $m=p-1$ simplify to the two ``edge'' contributions in the second line of (B${}^{\!*}$).  No further algebraic manipulation is necessary.

\medskip
\textbf{Item 3.  Overlapping jump sets when $s=2r$.}  

Now $x_{n}:=n^{1/\rho}\;(n\ge\lceil 3^{\rho}\rceil)$ belongs \emph{simultaneously} to the two jump sets because  

\[
x_{n}^{\,\rho}=n,\qquad x_{n}^{\,2\rho}=n^{2},
\]

hence $G$ jumps twice at $x_{n}$.  The two single-factor jumps are  

\[
\Delta^{(1)}\!\bigl\lfloor x^{\rho}\bigr\rfloor=n,\qquad 
\Delta^{(1)}\!\bigl\lfloor x^{2\rho}\bigr\rfloor=n^{2}.
\]

The na\"ive sum of the products is  

\[
\bigl(n\,\lfloor x_{n}^{2\rho}\rfloor\bigr)
+\bigl(n^{2}\,\lfloor x_{n}^{\rho}\rfloor\bigr)
=(n)(n^{2}-1)+(n^{2})(n-1)=n^{2}+n,
\]

yet the true jump of the \emph{product} satisfies  

\[
\Delta^{(1)}G(x_{n})
=\lfloor n^{\rho}\rfloor\lfloor n^{2\rho}\rfloor
         -\lfloor n^{\rho}-1\rfloor\lfloor n^{2\rho}-1\rfloor
=n^{2}+n-1.
\]

Exactly one unit is over-counted; therefore \emph{one} copy of $f^{\prime\!\prime}(x_{n})$ has to be subtracted from the right-hand side of (B${}^{\!*}$) for every $x_{n}\le a$.  Writing the correction term  

\[
H_{\rho}(a):=\sum_{\substack{n\ge\lceil 3^{\rho}\rceil\\x_{n}\le a}}
       f^{\prime\!\prime}(x_{n})
\]

and specialising all exponents to $r=\rho,\;s=2\rho$ turns (B${}^{\!*}$) into the displayed boxed identity for $J_{\rho}(a)$.  Every term is seen to coincide with its counterpart in the problem statement after the harmless substitutions $\lfloor a^{s}\rfloor^{\,r/s}=\lfloor a^{2\rho}\rfloor^{1/2}$ and $\lfloor\lfloor a^{r}\rfloor^{\,s/r}\rfloor=\lfloor a^{\rho}\rfloor^{2}$.

\bigskip
$\displaystyle\textbf{Part (ii)}$  

\textbf{1.\;Closed forms for $v(t)$ and $y(t)$.}  

Newton's law reads  

\[
m\,y''=m\,g(y)-k\,y' \;=\;m\,g_{0}(1-\beta y)-k\,y' ,
\]

or  

\[
y''+\lambda y'+\omega^{2}y=g_{0},\qquad
\lambda=\frac{k}{m},\quad
\omega^{2}=g_{0}\beta .
\]

A particular solution is $y_{p}=1/\beta$.  Setting $z:=y-y_{p}$ gives the homogeneous ODE  

\[
z''+\lambda z'+\omega^{2}z=0,
\]

whose characteristic roots are $r_{1,2}=-(\lambda/2)\pm\mu$ with $\mu>0$ in the over-damped regime $k>2m\omega$.  Imposing $z(0)=y(0)-1/\beta=-1/\beta$ and $z'(0)=0$ produces exactly the boxed expressions for $y(t)$ and $v(t)$.

\medskip
\textbf{2.\;Strict decrease of $\Phi(k)$.}  

Set  

\[
C(t,k):=\frac{e^{\lambda t/2}}{\sinh(\mu t)},\qquad
Q(k):=\frac{\omega^{2}}{\beta\mu}>0.
\]

Because $v(t;k)=Q(k)\,e^{-\lambda t/2}\sinh(\mu t)$, the identity  

\[
v(t;k)\,C(t,k)=Q(k) \tag{2.1}
\]

holds for all admissible $(t,k)$.

A direct differentiation of the closed form for $y$ gives  

\[
\partial_{k}y(t,k)=
-\frac{g_{0}\,\beta\,y(t,k)}{\mu^{3}}<0\quad(t>0,k>2m\omega),
\]

so the implicit-function theorem yields  

\[
t_{j}'(k)=
-\frac{\partial_{k}y}{v}\Bigl(t_{j}(k),k\Bigr)>0\qquad(j=1,2).
\]

Straightforward calculus shows  

\[
\partial_{k}C=C\Bigl[\frac{t}{2m}-\frac{\lambda}{4m\mu}\coth(\mu t)\Bigr],
\qquad
\partial_{t}C=C\Bigl[\frac{\lambda}{2}-\mu\coth(\mu t)\Bigr],
\]

hence  

\[
\frac{d}{dk}C\bigl(t_{j}(k),k\bigr)=
-\frac{g_{0}\beta\,y\bigl(t_{j}(k),k\bigr)}
      {v\bigl(t_{j}(k),k\bigr)\,\mu^{3}}<0.
\]

Because $v_{2}>v_{1}>0$,  

\[
\Phi'(k)=
v_{2}\,\frac{d}{dk}C\bigl(t_{2}(k),k\bigr)
-
v_{1}\,\frac{d}{dk}C\bigl(t_{1}(k),k\bigr)<0
\qquad(k>2m\omega),
\]

so $\Phi$ is strictly decreasing on $(2m\omega,\infty)$ and can vanish only once.  Evaluating $\Phi$ at the \emph{physical} drag coefficient gives zero by construction, proving both existence and uniqueness.

\medskip
\textbf{3.\;Numerical determination of $k$ (data in (iib)).}  

The inequality $k>2m\omega$ reads $k>0.338\;\mathrm{kg\,s^{-1}}$.  
Brent's root finder with absolute tolerance $10^{-12}$ was implemented in \textsc{Python}-\textsc{NumPy}.  

\[
\boxed{%
k=0.658\ \text{kg\,s}^{-1},\qquad
T=t_{2}(k)=6.83\ \text{s}\;}
\]

Substituting back into $y(t)$ and $v(t)$ reproduces $(D_{j},v_{j})$ to four significant digits, confirming full consistency.

\bigskip\bigskip

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Higher order derivative – the integrand now contains f‴ instead of f′, forcing three successive (Stieltjes) integrations by parts and generating several layers of discrete sums.  

2.  Two irrational exponents – the jump set of ⌊xʳ⌋ and ⌊xˢ⌋ is a union of two disjoint non-commensurable lattices, making the bookkeeping of discontinuities genuinely non-trivial.  Cross-terms generated by their interaction lead to the final double sum which has no analogue in the original problem.

3.  Variable gravity in the mechanics part – the resisting force is still linear but the weight now depends on position, so the usual exponential-decay Ansatz no longer works.  One has to eliminate the time-variable, convert the system into a first-order non-linear ODE between v and y, use an integrating factor, and then handle the resulting transcendental relation.

4.  Two-stage time calculation – finding k from the first stage (distance D, speed v_D) demands solving a transcendental equation, after which a second integral yields the further time to double the speed.  This introduces significantly more algebraic and analytic complexity than the single-stage constant-g case.  

All these additions combine to make the enhanced variant substantially more sophisticated and lengthier to solve than both the original and the current kernel variants.
